initial_claims.description: A malicious creditor can permanently block the payout queue by entering as a creditor with a contract address whose fallback always reverts or consumes more than 2300 gas. The contract uses a push-payment model and only increments lastCreditorPayedOut if the .send() succeeds. If the current queued creditor cannot receive ETH, every later call to lendGovernmentMoney() will keep attempting and failing to pay that same creditor, preventing progress for all subsequent creditors.
initial_claims.evidence: In lendGovernmentMoney(), when enough balance exists, the contract executes creditorAddresses[lastCreditorPayedOut].send(creditorAmounts[lastCreditorPayedOut]); then immediately subtracts buddies[...] and increments lastCreditorPayedOut += 1 without checking the send result. Since .send() returns false on failure and does not revert, a malicious contract creditor can force the transfer to fail forever. Because lastCreditorPayedOut remains unchanged, the same failing recipient stays at the head of the queue and blocks all future payouts. Exploit path: attacker deploys a contract with a reverting fallback, calls lendGovernmentMoney() from that contract with >= 1 ether to become a creditor, waits until it reaches the payout index, then all future payout attempts to that index fail and the queue is stuck.

claim.description: A malicious creditor can permanently block the payout queue by entering as a creditor with a contract address whose fallback always reverts or consumes more than 2300 gas. The contract uses a push-payment model and only increments lastCreditorPayedOut if the .send() succeeds. If the current queued creditor cannot receive ETH, every later call to lendGovernmentMoney() will keep attempting and failing to pay that same creditor, preventing progress for all subsequent creditors.
claim.evidence: In lendGovernmentMoney(), when enough balance exists, the contract executes creditorAddresses[lastCreditorPayedOut].send(creditorAmounts[lastCreditorPayedOut]); then immediately subtracts buddies[...] and increments lastCreditorPayedOut += 1 without checking the send result. Since .send() returns false on failure and does not revert, a malicious contract creditor can force the transfer to fail forever. Because lastCreditorPayedOut remains unchanged, the same failing recipient stays at the head of the queue and blocks all future payouts. Exploit path: attacker deploys a contract with a reverting fallback, calls lendGovernmentMoney() from that contract with >= 1 ether to become a creditor, waits until it reaches the payout index, then all future payout attempts to that index fail and the queue is stuck.

verdict.reasoning: The attacker’s exact queue-blocking mechanism is not supported by the code, but a real unchecked-send payout failure exists at the cited location. In `lendGovernmentMoney()`, the payout branch is: `if (creditorAmounts[lastCreditorPayedOut] <= address(this).balance - profitFromCrash) { creditorAddresses[lastCreditorPayedOut].send(creditorAmounts[lastCreditorPayedOut]); buddies[creditorAddresses[lastCreditorPayedOut]] -= creditorAmounts[lastCreditorPayedOut]; lastCreditorPayedOut += 1; }`. Because `.send()` in Solidity 0.4 returns `false` on failure and does not revert, and because the return value is ignored, the contract advances `lastCreditorPayedOut` even when no ETH was delivered. So the queue does NOT remain unchanged and is NOT permanently blocked as the attacker claimed. However, the underlying vulnerable pattern is concrete: a creditor can be skipped without payment by using a contract address whose fallback reverts or needs more than 2300 gas. This is physically achievable on mainnet: (1) attacker deploys a contract with reverting fallback, (2) calls `lendGovernmentMoney()` with at least 1 ether so their contract address is pushed into `creditorAddresses`, (3) when their index reaches `lastCreditorPayedOut` and sufficient balance exists, the `.send()` fails, but (4) the contract still decrements `buddies[...]` and increments `lastCreditorPayedOut`, permanently marking that creditor as paid when they were not. There is no code-level mitigation such as checking the boolean, reverting on failure, retry logic, or pull-based withdrawal. The defender correctly identified the actual mechanism and cited the relevant code behavior. Impact is limited: this does not DoS later creditors, but it does create silent payout failure and inconsistent accounting for the affected creditor. Under the stated rules, unchecked external call failures are valid findings even if the immediate harm is primarily to the caller’s own flow.
verdict.recommendation: Check the return value of `.send()` and only advance state on success, e.g. `bool ok = creditorAddresses[lastCreditorPayedOut].send(creditorAmounts[lastCreditorPayedOut]); if (!ok) { return true; }` before mutating `buddies` and `lastCreditorPayedOut`. Preferably replace push payments with a pull-payment pattern: record the amount owed and let each creditor withdraw their own funds. That fully avoids silent payout failure due to recipient fallback behavior.

There is no code-level mitigation such as checking the boolean, reverting on failure, retry logic, or pull-based withdrawal. The cited push-payment pattern is present, and there is no code-level mitigation that handles a failed payout to the current head creditor. In the payout path, the contract calls `.send()` to `creditorAddresses[lastCreditorPayedOut]` but does not check the returned boolean. Because `.send()` returns false instead of reverting on failure, execution continues and `lastCreditorPayedOut` is incremented regardless of whether the recipient actually received funds. This means the specific claim that the queue is blocked because `lastCreditorPayedOut` remains unchanged is inaccurate, but the underlying issue is still a denial-of-service / payout-failure vulnerability: a malicious creditor can force their payout to fail permanently and the contract will mark them as paid anyway, causing loss of liveness/fairness for that creditor. There is no mitigation such as checking the send result, retry logic, or pull-payment withdrawal.

The attacker’s exact queue-blocking mechanism is not supported by the code, but a real unchecked-send payout failure exists at the cited location. In `lendGovernmentMoney()`, the payout branch is: `if (creditorAmounts[lastCreditorPayedOut] <= address(this).balance - profitFromCrash) { creditorAddresses[lastCreditorPayedOut].send(creditorAmounts[lastCreditorPayedOut]); buddies[creditorAddresses[lastCreditorPayedOut]] -= creditorAmounts[lastCreditorPayedOut]; lastCreditorPayedOut += 1; }`. Because `.send()` in Solidity 0.4 returns `false` on failure and does not revert, and because the return value is ignored, the contract advances `lastCreditorPayedOut` even when no ETH was delivered. So the queue does NOT remain unchanged and is NOT permanently blocked as the attacker claimed. However, the underlying vulnerable pattern is concrete: a creditor can be skipped without payment by using a contract address whose fallback reverts or needs more than 2300 gas. This is physically achievable on mainnet: (1) attacker deploys a contract with reverting fallback, (2) calls `lendGovernmentMoney()` with at least 1 ether so their contract address is pushed into `creditorAddresses`, (3) when their index reaches `lastCreditorPayedOut` and sufficient balance exists, the `.send()` fails, but (4) the contract still decrements `buddies[...]` and increments `lastCreditorPayedOut`, permanently marking that creditor as paid when they were not. There is no code-level mitigation such as checking the boolean, reverting on failure, retry logic, or pull-based withdrawal.
